elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we consider nonsymmetric solutions to certain Lyapunov and Riccati equations and inequalities with coefficient matrices corresponding to cone-preserving dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Most results presented here appear to be novel even in the special case of positive systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: First, we provide a simple eigenvalue criterion for a Sylvester equation to admit a cone-preserving solution.
elements.sent-2411-004: For a single system preserving a self-dual cone, this reduces to stability.
elements.sent-2411-005: Further, we provide a set of conditions equivalent to testing a given H-infinity norm bound, as in the bounded real lemma.
elements.sent-2411-006: These feature the stability of a coefficient matrix similar to the Hamiltonian, a solution to two conic inequalities, and a stabilizing cone-preserving solution to a nonsymmetric Riccati equation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally, we show that the H-infinity norm is attained at zero frequency.
elements.sent-2411-008: A monotone linear dynamical system is a system such that an input $u(t)$ and a state initial condition $x(0)$ confined to a cone together imply that the state $x(t)$ and output $z(t)$ are also confined to a cone for all $t \geq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Although scarce and still at its infancy, research on the topic is warranted given the success of the special case in which the cones are taken as the nonnegative orthant.
elements.sent-2411-010: Such systems are known as positive systems and are particularly suited for analysis and synthesis of large-scale systems, e.g., \cite{farina2000positive}\cite{rantzer2018tutorial and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-011: One important reason is that $L_1$/$L_\infty$-gain verification and controller synthesis reduces to linear programming \cite{briat2013robust}\cite{rantzer2015scalable .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Similarly, positive diagonal solutions to Lyapunov equations and linear matrix inequalities (LMIs) are necessary and sufficient for achieving stability \cite{berman1994nonnegative and a given $H_\infty$ norm bound \cite{tanaka2011bounded , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The latter then paves the way for structured synthesis in which the sparsity pattern on the controller may be specified in exchange for requiring closed-loop positivity \cite{tanaka2011bounded .}
elements.sent-2411-014: An additional reformulation of the Lyapunov theorem and the bounded real lemma given in \cite{ebihara2014lmi is as follows: any nonsymmetric solution with positive definite symmetric part is necessary and sufficient in order to certify stability and a particular $H_\infty$ norm, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-015: By contrast, in standard Lyapunov and $H_\infty$ theory, symmetric solutions to Lyapunov equations \cite{rugh1996linear and LMIs \cite{boyd1994linear}\cite{gahinet1994lmi or Riccati equations \cite{doyle1988state are required to certify stability and performance, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: For general proper cones, the key notions appear to be cone-preservance and cross-positivity, corresponding to nonnegative matrices and Metzler matrices, respectively, see Section \ref{sec:prel .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the above results do not generalize in a straightforward way to general cone-preserving/monotone systems.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, extending the connection between the $L_1$ gain and linear programming in \cite{briat2013robust requires a shift to cone linear absolute norms in \cite{shen2017input .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Further, \cite[Chapter 4]{tanaka2012symmetric shows that the $H_\infty$ norm of a system which preserves a proper cone does not in general equal its static gain, as it does for positive systems \cite{rantzer2015scalable .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Instead, it is the spectral radius of a transfer function corresponding to such a system which achieves its maximum value at zero frequency \cite{tanaka2013dc .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Finally, the celebrated diagonal solution to Lyapunov equations and LMIs fails to hold more generally.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, if the solution is seen as the result of the quadratic representation associated with a Jordan Algebra applied to a vector obtained through a conic program, then the symmetric cones, a subset of the self-dual ones, appear to be the natural setting for this property.
elements.sent-2411-023: This is indicated by very recent works such as \cite{dalin2024special in the stability case, \cite{shen2016some in the bounded real lemma case and \cite{lu2024kyp in the KYP lemma case, the latter of which correspond to \cite{tanaka2011bounded and \cite{rantzer2015kalman , respectively, for positive systems.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Note in particular that the $H_\infty$ norm is shown to equal the static gain in this setting \cite{shen2016some .}
elements.sent-2411-025: By contrast, in this paper we explore what can be said when the proper cones are self-dual only.
elements.sent-2411-026: The flavor of our results is quite different from that of the above results, most notably in that we depart from matrix symmetry, let alone diagonality.
elements.sent-2411-027: In particular, we show that for two cross-positive matrices on a proper cone, the associated Sylvester equation admits a (possibly nonsymmetric) cone-preserving solution if and only if the sum of the greatest real parts of their eigenvalues is negative.
elements.sent-2411-028: It follows that when the cone is self-dual, a cone-preserving solution to the Lyapunov equation is necessary and sufficient for stability.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, we leverage a recent result in \cite{vladu2024riccati to provide a set of conditions which are equivalent to a $\gamma$-bound on the $H_\infty$ norm for monotone systems w.r.t.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Here, a) appears to generalize \cite{ebihara2014lmi above, whereas b), c) and d) are novel to the best of the authors' knowledge, even for positive systems.}
elements.sent-2411-031: For comparisons to similar results in the literature, see Remark \ref{rem:cf .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, we show that the symmetry of the state cone in \cite{shen2016some may in fact be relaxed to self-duality in order for the static gain to determine the $H_\infty$ norm.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The outline of the paper is as follows: Section \ref{sec:prel reviews the basics of cone theory and cross-positivity in particular, and Section \ref{sec:results presents the results of the paper.}}

elements.sent-2411-036: In this section, we explain our notation and supply the required background for the contents in the remaining sections.

elements.sent-2411-044: A set $K \subseteq \mathbb{R ^n$ is called a cone if $x \in \mathcal{K}$ and $\alpha \geq 0$ imply $\alpha x \in K$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: If in addition $K$ is convex, closed, pointed ($\mathcal{K \cap \mathcal{-K = \{0\}$) and has non-empty interior, then it is called a proper cone.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: A proper cone induces a partial order $\succeq_K$ on $\mathbb{R ^n$, i.e., $x \succeq_K y$ if and only if $x - y \in K$; strict inequality $x \succ_K y$ means that $x - y$ lies in the interior of $K$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The dual cone associated with a proper cone is \begin{equation}K_* = \{y \mid y^T x \geq 0 \; for \; all \; x \in K \}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}K_* = \{y \mid y^T x \geq 0 \; for \; all \; x \in K \}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-048: If $K = K_*$, then we say that $K$ is self-dual.
elements.sent-2411-049: A matrix $A \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$ is said to be cross-positive on $K$ if $x \in K$, $y \in K_*$ and $y^T x = 0$ imply $y^T A x \geq 0$.}

elements.sent-2411-179: In this paper, we have explored cone-preserving (possibly nonsymmetric) solutions to Lyapunov and Riccati equations and inequalities for the purpose of stability and performance verification.
elements.sent-2411-180: Overall, these results apply to LTI systems with zero direct term that are monotone w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-181: Given the latter's success, the main purpose of the paper has been to complement it with a new angle as well as to pin down the structure it hinges on: the self-duality of the nonnegative orthant.
elements.sent-2411-182: By contrast, previous results indicate that the celebrated diagonal solution property and its many consequences are generated by the symmetric cones, a subset of the self-dual ones.
elements.sent-2411-183: One concrete benefit of this distinction is that we may now attribute properties such as the $H_\infty$ norm being determined by the static gain to the former structure rather than the latter, instead of collapsing the two.
elements.sent-2411-184: Although the main contribution of the present paper is arguably one of understanding, it also hints at potential usage in areas such as controller synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-185: In particular, synthesis based on diagonal solutions as in \cite{tanaka2011bounded gives $H_\infty$ optimality only for the restricted set of stabilizing closed-loop positive controllers.}
elements.sent-2411-186: In a worst-case scenario, this optimal value may be very far from the optimal value over the entire set of stabilizing controllers.
elements.sent-2411-187: By contrast, in Subsection \ref{subsec:example_irrigation , we see how closed-loop positive controllers are featured quite naturally as optimal also within this wider framework.}
